denial_pattern_sources: timeline_violation: DOL ERISA Claims Procedure Regulations 29 CFR § 2560.503-1; documented in UHC state AG complaints 2022–2024
incorrect_demographic_guideline: Commonwealth Fund 2023 Review of Prior Auth Denials
denial_letter_text:
Dear Plan Member,

We have completed our review of the request for Catheter ablation for symptomatic AFib related to Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (I48.0).

After clinical review using UnitedHealthcare Medical Policies and Optum clinical criteria, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: Insurer requires failure of two class III antiarrhythmics before ablation. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria (MCG guidelines, current edition not specified) for medical necessity determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Insurer says patient must try cheaper alternative X first.

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize Catheter ablation for symptomatic AFib. If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management

Please note that standard criteria apply regardless of prior submissions.
clinical_context:
The 55-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (I48.0). She has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended Catheter ablation for symptomatic AFib as the next appropriate step.

Patient failed dofetilide and sotalol with documented rhythm logs. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on step therapy missing without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.
